A memorial honoring one of the most horrific US war crimes in modern history had about 30 people in attendance at once. But perhaps that most honestly memorialized the attack. A moving attempt to place a physical display of criminality at the footsteps of where the crime originates: a building where politicians mill around as if it’s just another day, as if the screams of an Iranian child are as detectable as a momentary gust of wind.

And that harsh contrast was all the more evident as I departed. After the House finished voting for the day, I ran into Utah Republican Rep. Burgess Owens and asked him how Congress was responding to the strike. “I don’t know,” he said plainly.

I then asked how the US should respond. He then asked me, a journalist who has no power over foreign policy, how I am responding to the Iranian government killing what he said was 40,000 people. Here’s the interaction:

Minutes later, Senator Lindsey Graham took to the Senate floor to speak in support of the war.

He said the best way to support service members was to have them “finish the job,” no matter, apparently, the cost.

“American people: there’s going to be some pain. The region: you’re going to get hit again. We’ve lost soldiers. God bless them. God bless the wounded, couple hundred. And casualties may increase.”

Shortly thereafter, the news broke: Pete Hegseth’s Pentagon wants $200 billion more for the war.

I began the day at a harrowing tribute to the worst of our capabilities. I ended it witnessing one Republican consider dead children as fodder for a debate, and another treat service members as chess pieces and Iranian civilians as even less.

The drums of war banged loudly. The victims’ screams echoed into oblivion.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iranian officials said last week that at least 1,444 people have been killed and nearly 20,000 injured in the US-Israeli strikes – the latest updated figures from the country. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency today put the death toll at more than 3,000, including over 1,369 civilian deaths.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 968 people, including more than 115 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 15 have been killed and thousands of others injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks in Israel.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region.

And in more Iran war news…

Trump criticizes Israel, but threatens Iran: Trump claimed the US knew nothing about Israel’s attack yesterday on Iran’s South Pars gas field. Israel “violently lashed out,” Trump said. The Israeli attack prompted Iran to strike energy sites across the Gulf, including in Qatar, the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Kuwait. Trump warned, “NO MORE ATTACKS WILL BE MADE BY ISRAEL” on South Pars unless Iran attacked Qatar’s energy sites again, which would prompt the US to “massively blow up the entirety” of the gas field.

Oil prices soar: The attacks on Gulf oil facilities sent the price of Brent crude soaring to $118 a barrel before settling at about $107.

Revenge vow: Paying tribute to Ali Larijani, the Iranian Supreme National Security Council secretary killed on Tuesday, Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei said: “Every blood has a price that the criminal murderers of the martyrs must soon pay.”

Big budget ask: The US Department of Defense wants the White House to approve a request to Congress for more than $200 billion to fund operations against Iran, the Washington Post reported. One senior official told the paper some Trump officials think Congress will not approve such a figure.

Palestinian women killed in West Bank: Three Palestinian women, including a teenager, were killed when falling shrapnel hit a hair salon near Hebron in the occupied West Bank. The women are the first known Palestinian fatalities in the West Bank related to the US-Israeli war on Iran (Israel blamed an Iranian missile, while Palestinians blamed an errant Israeli aerial defense interceptor). Palestinians in the West Bank do not have the same kinds of warning systems or shelters that Jewish Israelis have.

Lebanon latest: More than 45 people have been killed in Israeli attacks in the last 48 hours, the country’s Health Ministry said, as Israel escalates its bombardment and pushes deeper into Lebanon. More than 1 million people have been displaced.

Jones Act waived: Attempting to combat rising prices caused by the war, the Trump administration temporarily waived the 1920 law that requires goods moving between US ports to be carried by ships owned and crewed by Americans. Experts were skeptical, one telling NPR that rocketing gasoline prices are based on world markets and have “nothing to do with the Jones Act.”

Mullin’ a New Gig

Oklahoma Republican Markwayne Mullin testified in front of the Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee on Wednesday, as he seeks to become the next Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security. It did not go well.

Committee chair Rand Paul set the tone immediately. The Kentucky Republican pressed Mullin on calling him a “freaking snake” in the past, why Mullin could side with Paul’s neighbor, who assaulted him years ago, leaving him with broken ribs, and how the American people could trust him.

“Explain to the American people why they should trust a man with anger issues to set the proper example for ICE and border patrol agents.”

Paul also questioned Mullin on past comments he made praising an episode in 1856, when pro-slavery Rep. Preston Brooks caned Senator Charles Sumner, crushing his skull and knocking him unconscious. Mullin responded by saying that “dueling with two consenting adults” is still a rule that applies to Congress (a lie, and also an incredible retort in response to the Sumner caning).

Elsewhere, Mullin refused to apologize for saying the killing of Renee Good was justified, and did not apologizefor calling Alex Pretti a “deranged individual,” only going as far as to say he regretted saying it.

While Mullin may not be able to count on the support of Paul, he will need just one Democrat to save his bid out of committee. He may be in luck: shortly after Mullin’s nomination was first announced, Fetterman said he planned to vote in favor.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

DNI dodges: In a statement released before a Senate hearing, Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard placed herself at odds with Trump, saying strikes last year “obliterated” Iran’s nuclear enrichment program, with “no efforts” made to rebuild. But when Gabbard spoke before the intelligence committee, she left that part out, later claiming to have “skipped” it to save time.

Labor icon accused: The New York Times reported extensive allegations that the labor activist César Chavez, who died in 1993, sexually abused women and girls. Dolores Huerta, 95 and herself a revered labor leader, described being “manipulated and pressured” by Chavez, and “forced against my will.”

Platner responds: The Maine Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner responded to attacks from his opponent, Governor Janet Mills, concerning comments about rape he made on online forums. In a 30-second ad, Platner asked voters “not to judge me for the worst thing I said on the internet on my worst day 14 years ago.”

Kashing in: FBI Director Kash Patel told senators his agency is buying information that can be used to track people’s movement and location history, a practice he said was both lawful and producing “valuable intelligence.” Senator Ron Wyden (D-Oregon) accused Patel of “an outrageous end run” around constitutional privacy protections.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

US gas prices have soared by 27% in the past four weeks. In what three states has the price of a gallon of gas increased by more than a dollar?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: At least four Palestinians were killed in two separate Israeli strikes on Gaza City today. Meanwhile, the Global Sumud Flotilla announced new plans to send its “largest mission yet” to Gaza. The flotilla will include more than 100 boats that will set sail in “parallel with land mobilizations,” with representatives from over 100 countries.

🇦🇫🇵🇰 Temporary truce: Pakistan and Afghanistan agreed to temporarily pause the violence that has killed hundreds, to mark Eid al-Fitr. The cessation of hostilities from late Wednesday to midnight Tuesday was requested by Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Turkey.

🇸🇩 Over a dozen killed: At least 17 people died, and 123 were wounded amid renewed fighting on the border between Sudan and Chad, Doctors Without Borders said. The Sudanese army has been at war with the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces since April 2023.

🇨🇩🇷🇼 Another promise to de-escalate: After meeting for two days in the US, Congolese and Rwandan officials agreed on steps to de-escalate tensions in eastern Congo, where Congolese troops have been fighting rebels backed by Rwanda.

😳 WTF?!

Would you believe us if we told you that this guy used to run the FBI? In fact, he was the agency’s second-in-command…

Trump will literally hire just about anyone these days.

🧠 Trivia answer: New Mexico (+$1.18), Arizona (+$1.17), and Colorado (+$1.06).

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

item.title: CBS Owner Paramount Pushed Pro-Israel Panels While Squashing One on Gaza
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait’! Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest dispatches from Bari World and the right-wing industrial outrage complex. In tonight’s edition, I’ve got a scoop on Paramount axing a panel discussion about Gaza from the perspective of Arab…
item.author: Ragebait | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait’!

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest dispatches from Bari World and the right-wing industrial outrage complex. In tonight’s edition, I’ve got a scoop on Paramount axing a panel discussion about Gaza from the perspective of Arab Americans. Plus, we look at how the New York Times is “sanewashing” the president’s unhinged ramblings – again; and the ridiculous spin that CBS News is giving Tony Dokoupil’s ratings crash.

Let’s jump in.

Paramount Canceled Arab-American Gaza Panel at the Last Minute

CBS owner Paramount squashed an internal panel discussion on the Arab-American experience amid the Gaza war that featured designer and prominent pro-Palestine activist Alana Hadid – all while holding multiple pro-Israel sessions, Ragebait has learned.

Among the three separate sessions Paramount held at its annual global Inclusion Week in October 2024 that focused on antisemitism was an in-depth panel by Israeli-flag-waving CBS reporterJonah Kaplan and featuring pro-Israel self-proclaimed “MAGA lefty” provocateur Batya Ungar-Sargon.

This all occurred just two weeks after Paramount’s owner rallied to the defense of Tony Dokoupil for outrageously paintingTa-Nehisi Coates as a terror-sympathizing extremist over the celebrated author’s sympathetic stance towards Palestine.

Based on my conversations with five former and current Paramount employees, as well as a review of communications and documents that I obtained, I’ve learned that the company’s Office of Global Inclusion (OGI) – which has since been disbanded as part of Paramount’s anti-DEI pledge to the Trump administration – nixed the Arab-American discussion on Gaza less than two days before it was scheduled to take place.

A representative for Paramount declined to comment.

For several years, Paramount’s Inclusion Week gave the entertainment giant’s employees access to dozens of virtual and in-person sessions that the company touted as reaffirming its “commitment to making Paramount a place of belonging.” This included a 2023 panel titled “The American Jewish Experience Amidst Rising Antisemitism,” which featured Kaplan and then-‘CBS Saturday Morning’ anchor Dana Jacobson. That year’s event, meanwhile, did not offer a similar Palestinian or Muslim perspective.

Weeks before the 2024 Inclusion Week, with Israel’s war in Gaza having already left tens of thousands of Palestinians dead while anti-war protests were being equated to antisemitism, an employee resource group known as MOSAIC that represents Paramount staffers of Southwest Asian, North African, and Middle Eastern heritage proposed a panel that could bring the Palestinian perspective into light for the company’s staff.

Beginning in late September, a debate raged over the type of session the employee group would be allowed to present at the corporate event. This included OGI expressing “some concerns” about some of the panelists initially slated to appear and even urging the employees to scrap its plans for a panel altogether after a proposed “fireside chat” featuring Rep. Rashida Tlaib fell apart.

Determined to press forward, the employees said it was imperative to schedule a panel following the company’s “one-sided approach” at 2023’s Inclusion Week and the multiple sessions already devoted to antisemitism. They also noted that a session highlighting the Arab-American voice had “become so much more urgent and crucial” following Dokoupil’s interview with Coates.

Then-Paramount chair Shari Redstone, a passionate supporter of pro-Israel causes who had just blasted CBS for reprimanding Dokoupil for his interview with Coates, ended up holding a “special fireside chat” at 2024’s event.

By the middle of October, details had finally been hashed out to present “The Arab American Experience Amidst the Crisis in Gaza,” which would feature Hadid, Rutgers Law School professor Sahar Aziz, Palestinian musician Anees, and then-TRT World producer Sumayya Tobah as moderator.

“Our approach during Inclusion Week was to exactly emulate what they’ve done for the antisemitism and Jewish panels,” one source familiar with the matter told me. “To just be like, ‘Well, since you’re talking about the same region and the same conflicts, can we please just talk about the other side?’”

Six days later, after the employee group began notifying employees about the upcoming panel, some panelists reached out to the group for updates, stating they’d received phone calls explaining that the session had been postponed. By the morning of Oct. 22, the panel had been removed from the Inclusion Week schedule.

According to sources familiar with the matter, OGI informed the group’s leadership team that the panel was canceled due to social media activity by some of its participants related to Israel and the October 7 attacks. In a follow-up email to MOSAIC, Paramount’s inclusion office positioned the cancellation as a postponement until a new moderator and panelists could be found and vetted.

OGI also sent emails to employees who had registered to attend and participate in the session, apologizing for the “inconvenience.” In response to employees’ disappointment about the cancellation, the office sent a canned response.

“Thank you for your inquiry around Inclusion Week and the Arab American Experience Amidst the Crisis in Gaza session. Unfortunately, as with other sessions that were originally scheduled for this year’s Inclusion Week programming, this panel was postponed,” the email, which I received multiple copies of, stated.

“As we’ve noted previously, we will continue to offer additional sessions beyond this week,” the email continued. “And we are moving forward with plans to offer programming specific to the Arab American experience before the end of the year.”

Neither the panel nor any other “Arab American experience” would be offered to Paramount employees by the end of 2024, or at any time thereafter, according to the staffers I spoke with.

Meanwhile, sources told me that the “What’s Old Is New Again: The Language of Antisemitism” panel led by Kaplan – who was recently promoted to network correspondent by CBS News editor-in-chiefBari Weiss – was allowed to continue and was “deeply problematic,” not least of which was due to the participation of Ungar-Sargon. (The other panelists were CBS News lead booker Kaci Sokoloff and Rabbi David Wolpe.)

Ungar-Sargon, a Trump sycophant who once falsely claimed she was protested by pro-Palestinian activists simply “for being a Jew,” suggested during the panel that anti-war protesters chanting “from the river to the sea” were ostensibly advocating for mass rape, murder, and kidnapping.

“And so, for our panel to get canceled – and they specifically told us it was because of the social media activity of the panelists – for her to be allowed to speak after all the crazy nonsense she said about Palestine and Gaza seemed hypocritical,” one Paramount staffer told me.

“There was stuff shared in that panel that I personally took offense to,” the staffer added. “I spoke to HR about it and was told under no uncertain terms that we shouldn’t be looking for equity and parity with what that group or that panel was allowed to do.”

In the 17 months since that panel was canceled, Redstone sold Paramount to David Ellison — and I think we all know how much more pro-Israel the company has become since then.

NYT Sanewashing Trump – Again

As we’ve noted here time and time again, the New York Times has a habit of “sanewashing” Donald Trump, especially as the president has grown increasingly erratic, unhinged, and incoherent over the years. At the same time, while going out of its way to soften its coverage of Trump, the Gray Lady has taken other prominent politicians to task over their verbal stumbles.

Following yet another rambling news conference by the president, ‘The Daily Show’ producer Matt Negrin called out the Times by noting how it highlighted Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez’s struggles at a foreign policy conference, comparing that to their coverage of Trump’s latest responses.

“Last month the NYT said AOC ‘struggled’ to give an answer on Taiwan and quoted her with ‘uhs’ and ‘ums’ to make her look stupid,” Negrin posted on social media, along with screenshotsof the Times’ articles on AOC’s and Trump’s remarks. “Yesterday talking about Hormuz Trump claimed he’s heard from ‘numerous countries’ and said ‘uh’ four times. The NYT deleted the ‘uhs’ and paraphrased him to look smarter.”

“The strongest ally Trump and Republicans have is stealth right-wing media rewriting history before it can become history,” Negrin concluded.

“We often omit extraneous syllables from quotations like ‘um’s’ and ‘ah’s,’ when not relevant to the news being reported, but if a politician’s quote is particularly striking for how stalled or jumbled it was, we sometimes reflect it in full to show the reader,” a New York Times spokesperson said in a statement, adding that the paper has “covered Trump’s meandering and rambling manner of speech extensively.”

Of course, when the Times isn’t line-editing Trump’s rants to make him sound more coherent and stable, they are rationalizing his irrational behavior. Case in point – this Style section piece framing the president forcing his lackeys to wear ill-fitting shoes as something that’s not “relegated to the Trump administration” and merely part of male behavior.

You Spin Me Right Round, Baby, Right Round

Well, that didn’t take long.

Just two months after Tony Dokoupil took over the anchor seat of Weiss’s MAGA-friendly revamp of ‘CBS Evening News,’ the Tiffany Network’s flagship nightly news broadcast – once the home of Walter Cronkite and Dan Rather – has sunk to new ratings lows.

Despite Trump’s illegal war of choice in Iran driving the news cycle, ‘CBS Evening News’ dipped below 4 million viewers for the first time this year. According to Nielsen, the Dokoupil-led program averaged just 3.845 million total viewers and 468,000 in the key 25-54 advertising demographic last week. And that’s after the network “retitled” Friday’s broadcast so that it wouldn’t count as part of the show’s ratings for the week.

In comparison, ABC’s ‘World News Tonight’ with David Muir drew 8.4 million viewers while ‘NBC Nightly News’ with Tom Llamas pulled in 6.5 million.

Still, while the plummeting viewership places Dokoupil in the same unenviable spot that spurred Paramount to pull the plug on the show’s two-anchor experiment late last year, the network is spinning like a top to defend the ratings crash.

“CBS News executives ascribe some portion of the viewership results to changes tied to the recent shift to Daylight Savings Time, according to a person familiar with the matter,” Variety’s Brian Steinberg reported on Tuesday night. He added that the execs were also “encouraged” that the program’s viewership was slightly up when “compared to the average viewership of the current season to date.”

Besides outright laughing over the “Daylight Savings Time” excuse, one media industry expert dismissed CBS News touting “season to date” metrics for the evening news telecast.

“News doesn’t have seasons,” the expert told me. “When was the last time they ran a repeat of the evening news… oh wait – never! You can’t do seasons when it’s news. Although A for effort, I guess.”

Meanwhile, reacting to the sinking audience (and reputation) of Dokoupil’s version of ‘CBS Evening News,’ one network reporter told me the following: “Is it bad that many of us are relishing in the failures of this place, which proves that the direction they are taking is destroying the legacy of this organization?”

A representative for CBS News did not respond to a request for comment.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘Ragebait’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘Ragebait’ off.

item.title: I Wargamed Iran for Obama. This Is the Worst-Case Scenario
item.description: Earlier this week, Donald Trump was asked whether he was surprised that no one briefed him in advance that Iran might respond to an American attack by targeting Gulf countries. His response? “Nobody. Nobody. No no no.” That is evidence that either Trump is lying or his administration has so cut…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Earlier this week, Donald Trump was asked whether he was surprised that no one briefed him in advance that Iran might respond to an American attack by targeting Gulf countries. His response? “Nobody. Nobody. No no no.”

That is evidence that either Trump is lying or his administration has so cut itself off from experts that no one briefed him on what was essentially common wisdom amongst US experts – that major strikes on Iran could prompt Tehran to respond by targeting oil infrastructure and US bases across the Middle East and closing the Strait of Hormuz.

For the past 15 years, I’ve participated in and helped run scenario exercises and war games on Iran both at the Pentagon and then outside of government. These exercises are not about predicting the future with certainty. They are about understanding the possible consequences of choices.

And on Iran, the lessons from these exercises were remarkably consistent and quite similar to what we’ve seen play out over the past two weeks. Here are five things Trump should have known and would have known if his administration had listened to experts:

1. When the regime feels existentially threatened, Iran will escalate

In most war games, conflicts began at a lower level: an Israeli strike, a proxy attack, a miscalculation. At that stage, Iran usually calibrated its response. It retaliated, but tried to avoid drawing the United States fully into the conflict because it knew that a full-on war with the US represented a massive threat. That pattern held in real life during the Israel-Iran exchanges of 2024 and 2025.

But once the United States entered directly, and once the regime believed its survival was at stake, those restraints would disappear.

At that point, Iran’s playbook was well understood: strike US bases, harass shipping in the Gulf, activate proxy forces across the region, and target oil infrastructure and civilian assets. The goal was to impose costs, spike oil prices, and create international pressure on Washington to stop.

This was not hard to foresee. It was one of the most consistent conclusions in the exercises I saw. What we are watching now was entirely predictable.

2. It is easy to close the Strait of Hormuz. It is hard to reopen it

One of the central lessons from these scenarios was always how easy it was for Iran to disrupt the Strait of Hormuz because of its geographic proximity and how much harder it is for the United States to restore confidence and safe passage.

We used to assume that a full closure – especially with mines – would be a last resort because it would also block Iran’s own exports. But what this war has shown is that Iran does not actually need to shut the strait entirely. It only needs to create enough uncertainty through drones, missile attacks, threats, and selective disruption to scare off shipping and drive up prices.

At the same time, Iran has still managed to move significant amounts of oil, especially to China. That means it can inflict pain on the global economy while still generating revenue for itself.

When I studied such scenarios and brought in economists and energy experts, we concluded that a major war could send oil prices soaring up to $175-$200 per barrel for a couple of months and leave a lasting risk premium on global markets with prices at $80-$100 long after the fighting stopped. That basic logic still holds.

3. Russia always wins

Whenever a Russia team was part of these games, they usually sat back and benefited.

That is exactly what is happening now. Higher oil prices benefit Russia as a major exporter. Pressure to stabilize global markets eases sanctions on Russian energy sales. And American attention, military resources, and political bandwidth that might otherwise go toward supporting Ukraine are redirected to the Middle East.

For Vladimir Putin, this is close to ideal: more revenue, less pressure, and a distracted United States.

None of that is surprising to anyone who has spent time thinking seriously about this kind of conflict.

4. The aftermath is incredibly costly

After Iraq, US policymakers understood that the real danger was not just how a war starts, but what comes after. So we spent a great deal of time gaming out post-conflict scenarios for Iran.

The conclusion was sobering. Short of a full-scale invasion and regime change – which was simply too costly – an air and naval war would likely leave the regime in place: weakened, damaged, and humiliated, but still standing. Its military would be degraded. Its economy and infrastructure battered. But the regime would survive, and it would almost certainly become more aggressive and more willing to take risks.

In that world, the United States then faces the prospect of containing a wounded but still dangerous Iran for years. Think of Iraq in the 1990s after the first Gulf War: a hostile regime that survived and required an expensive, long-term American military commitment to keep boxed in.

The difference is that today the United States does not have the same freedom of action as the only global superpower that it had in the 1990s. China is a major competitor. Russia remains a serious threat. We do not have the luxury of getting bogged down again in a prolonged Middle East containment mission.

5. Buying time was always the best option

What these war games ultimately taught me was that when it comes to Iran, all the options were bad, but war was among the worst. The smarter alternative was to contain, deter, pressure, negotiate, and wait for 86-year-old Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei to die and see if a more pragmatic or moderate leadership followed.

That could take different forms. Barack Obama tried diplomacy through the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action, which successfully constrained Iran’s program until Trump walked away from the deal. More recently, in the aftermath of the brutal crackdown on protesters, there were options to intensify sanctions enforcement, increase Iran’s isolation, and exploit the regime’s internal mounting domestic dissatisfaction to let the regime crumble from within.

By escalating to war, killing Khamenei, and forcing a leadership transition under conditions of extreme threat to the regime, the United States and Israel ensured that at the moment of truth, the dominant imperative inside Iran was not any kind of rethinking but instead simply survival. And so it is no surprise that the outcome is a new hardline leader close to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. A historic opportunity lost.

The bottom line is simple.

A major US-Iran war is not some unknowable black box. People have spent decades studying it, gaming it, and thinking through its consequences. The conclusion has been consistent: it would be enormously costly for Iran, for the United States, for the Middle East, and for the global economy.

There are no real winners in such a war – except, perhaps, Vladimir Putin.

That is why, for decades, presidents of both parties listened to experts and tried to avoid it until Trump ended all of that.

Ilan Goldenberg is the senior vice president and chief policy officer at J Street. He served as Iran team chief in the Office of the Secretary of Defense during the Obama administration. Subscribe to the Word on the Street Substack for more of Ilan’s writing.

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: AIPAC Strikes Back
item.description: On this day in 1969, the Nixon administration began carpet bombing Cambodia. Today, our president is once again a madman and drops bombs “just for fun.” Good morning! Andrew here, taking stock of what happened in last night’s congressional primary races in Illinois. Money might not automatically…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1969, the Nixon administration began carpet bombing Cambodia. Today, our president is once again a madman and drops bombs “just for fun.”

Good morning! Andrew here, taking stock of what happened in last night’s congressional primary races in Illinois. Money might not automatically buy elections – but, boy, are Democratic voters gonna get conned if they listen to any of AIPAC’s super PACs about which candidates are big-time supporters of Medicare for All and which left-wing candidates are actually secret Republicans.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Tuesday’s elections showcase America’s acute democracy crisis, Israel knows protesters in Iran will be “slaughtered” if they listen to Israel and rise up, Graham Platner faces his first attack ad, and Chief Justice John Roberts wishes he could tell the fascist he made president to stop criticizing the Supreme Court – but is it really safe to upset him?

What We’re Up Against

The week began with an NBC News poll finding that 67% of Democrats say their sympathies lie with Palestinians more than Israelis – a giant shift from 2013, when only 18% said the same.

Public opinion is no small matter. It’s not necessarily what decides elections, though, in an era of unprecedented, unlimited dark money and super PAC spending. To be sure, US policymaking has had no correlation with what Americans want for years, at least – the seminal study showing this was published back in 2014. But America’s democracy crisis is only getting more acute, as the ultra-rich and well-heeled interest groups have been given the greenest light, legally speaking, to mess with our elections.

Today, the American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC), the US pro-Israel lobby, can drop $22 million into four congressional primaries, as it did in Illinois, presenting fake and misleading political arguments to voters, and cynically attacking disfavored candidates from the left, while choosing to talk exclusively about topics that have zero relation to the primary issue that AIPAC and its donors care about: Israel.

AIPAC and its allies, through a series of anodyne-named super PACs, spent $22 million on four Illinois congressional primaries, and won two of them. And while AIPAC’s preferred candidate lost in Illinois’s 9th congressional district, the group still managed to block out the strongly pro-Palestine and Palestinian-American candidate Kat Abughazaleh.

In its campaign against Abughazaleh, who wants to end weapons sales to Israel, an AIPAC-aligned super PAC ran ads saying she “supported Marco Rubio for president, said she was to the right of Donald Trump on Social Security, and her campaign is bankrolled by right-wing donors to Trump and Republicans. Who is the real Kat Abughazaleh? We don’t really know.”

Keep in mind: AIPAC is funded by right-wing Trump donors – and if Abughazaleh, a progressive influencer and former Zeteo contributor, thought any of those things today, or was taking right-wing donor money, AIPAC would not have spent money against her.

But this is the power of the super PAC: add money and you can scream whatever bullshit you want through Americans’ TVs, computers, and iPhones. Your budget for trolling is limited only by the size of your donors’ trusts.

In Abughazaleh’s case, an AIPAC-aligned super PAC called the Chicago Progressive Partnership spent $1.2 million on ads attacking her. The same super PAC also spent $105,000 to prop up Bushra Amiwala – a Muslim candidate whom AIPAC did not want to win – and siphon away support from Abughazaleh.

One of the ads declared Amiwala to be “the real deal,” praising her “economic justice” platform: “Medicare for All, a living wage, student loan forgiveness, real affordable housing options, and yes, making the wealthy pay their fair share.”

The Chicago Progressive Partnership only formed on Jan. 22. It has not yet filed a report detailing its donors.

Abughazaleh finished second to Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss, who was backed by Senator Elizabeth Warren, the Congressional Progressive Caucus, and J Street. Abughazaleh appears to have lost by under four points, while Amiwala finished with about 5% of the vote – a spread that will surely generate angst among the left.

AIPAC initially spent millions in the race backing Laura Fine, who finished third, and opposing Biss, a liberal Zionist who said he wanted to condition aid to Israel.

Late in the race, AIPAC apparently took a lesson from its recent failure in New Jersey, where its super PAC undercut a somewhat pro-Israel, establishment Democrat and helped throw the election to a progressive.

AIPAC changed course, and began focusing on drawing away support from Abughazaleh.

A source close to AIPAC described its thinking in Illinois to Jewish Insider on Monday: “the group’s main goal in the primaries is to prevent six candidates – state Sen. Robert Peters in the 2nd District, activist Kina Collins in the 7th, activist Junaid Ahmed and Hanover Park Trustee Yasmeen Bankole in the 8th and influencer Kat Abughazaleh and Skokie School Board member Bushra Amiwala in the 9th – from being elected, as it believes those candidates would be aligned with the far-left ‘Squad’ on Israel policy issues if elected to Congress.”

That note may involve some goalpost shifting regarding the 9th district, to be sure, but none of these candidates won.

Junaid Ahmed lost by five points after the Chicago Progressive Partnership ran ads with the message: “Ahmed is a multi-millionaire who made his fortune on tech stocks, renting expensive properties, and investing in Elon Musk’s company. Ahmed consulted for a pharmaceutical company accused of jacking up prices for life-saving medicine, and he worked for a firm that advised fossil fuel companies as climate change worsened. Junaid Ahmed. He got rich. We got screwed.”

Ahmed, who had between $1,000 and $15,000 invested in Tesla as of 2022, ran on a firmly progressive platform, with support from Senator Bernie Sanders. He backed Medicare for All, wanted to leverage “Medicare’s buying power to bring down prescription drug prices for everyone,” and pushed for “a path to 100% clean energy.”

He also called Israel’s genocide in Gaza a genocide – so AIPAC’s super PAC set out to destroy him from the left by trolling the voters in his district.

Democratic voters may have moved left on Israel and Palestine. But a Democratic Party so dependent on dark money hasn’t followed them. This is what a crisis of representative democracy looks like.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iranian officials said last week that at least 1,444 people have been killed and nearly 20,000 injured in the US-Israeli strikes – the latest official updated figures from the country. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency today put the death toll at more than 3,000, including over 1,354 civilian deaths.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 912 people, including more than 110 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s health ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: The death toll in Israel rose to at least 14 after an elderly couple was killed in an Iranian strike in a Tel Aviv suburb.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region.

And in more Iran war news…

Senior Iranian officials killed: Israel said today that an Israeli attack killed Iranian Intelligence Minister Esmail Khatib (Iran has yet to comment on the claim). Israel’s claim comes after Iran confirmed late yesterday its security chief, Ali Larijani, and General Gholam Reze Soleimani, head of the volunteer Basij force, were killed in Israeli airstrikes.

Lebanon latest: Israel intensified its bombardment of Lebanon today, hitting central Beirut with some of the most intense strikes in that part of the capital in decades. At least 10 people were killed in Israeli strikes that targeted residential buildings. In southern Lebanon, Israel has reportedly killed several others, including a paramedic. Meanwhile, Israel acknowledged today that its forces fired on UN peacekeeping positions earlier this month in an attack that injured Ghanaian peacekeepers.

Israel predicts ‘slaughter’: Senior Israeli officials told their US counterparts that although Israel is publicly calling for a popular uprising in Iran, it believes protesters will “get slaughtered,” the Washington Post reported, citing a State Department cable.

Trump needs no one: After world leaders loudly rejected US efforts to build a naval coalition to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, Trump now claims he never needed the help of NATO or other partner countries. “WE DON’T NEED THE HELP OF ANYONE!” he declared.

Hunger risk rises: The UN World Food Program warned that almost 45 million more people could face acute food security or worse if the Iran conflict does not end by the middle of the year and if oil stays above $100 a barrel.

Military matters: US officials told Reuters that a fire aboard the US aircraft carrier Gerald R. Ford caused nearly 200 smoke-related injuries and prompted the giant ship to temporarily pull into port during its deployment in the Red Sea.

Mills Goes Negative in Maine

Down in the polls with less than three months left in the Maine Senate primary, Democratic Governor Janet Mills unleashed a blistering attack ad against Graham Platner – confirming the veteran and oyster farmer is, indeed, the frontrunner in the race to take on Republican Senator Susan Collins.

The ad features a speaker whose gravelly voice sounds eerily similar to Platner (Mills’s campaign says it was taped by a voice actor, and wasn’t made with AI). The speaker reads from ugly, misogynistic Reddit posts that the veteran and oyster farmer made in 2013. The ad shows several women responding to the posts, and ends with footage of Platner shirtless, sporting the infamous skull-and-crossbones tattoo – the one resembling a Nazi symbol – that he had covered up last year.

“When I read through my old internet posts when they resurfaced six months ago, I was horrified,” Platner said at a press conference with supporters Tuesday. “I did not recognize in them myself or the man that I am today. I didn’t recognize myself in this person that was struggling to find meaning and posted awful things 13 years ago. I am sorry. But it does not in any way reflect who I am today or the beliefs that I hold.”

🗞️ What You Need to Know

‘Dangerous’ president: Speaking in Houston, Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts seemed to clap back against Trump’s recent attacks by saying: “Personally directed hostility is dangerous and it’s got to stop.” Trump’s latest tantrum came after the Supreme Court struck down his illegal tariffs, leading the president to call the Roberts court “inept and embarrassing.”

Jersey rules: District court Judge Zahid Quraishi reached his wits’ end with the New Jersey US attorney’s office during a hearing, demanding answers on former interim US attorney (and Trump lawyer) Alina Habba’s ongoing involvement in the office before ultimately ejecting senior prosecutor Mark Coyne from the courtroom. Judge Quraishi then called for three officials to testify about who’s really running the NJ office.

Bondi to the front: House Oversight Chair James Comer subpoenaed Attorney General Pam Bondi to testify as part of the committee’s investigation into “possible mismanagement” of the investigation into Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell. Both Democrats and Republicans pushed for the move.

Markwayne his words: Oklahoma GOP Senator Markwayne Mullin will face the Senate Homeland Security Committee today, for his hearing to replace Kristi Noem as DHS Secretary, as the department grinds through a fifth week in shutdown. Democrats are likely to press Mullin on demands for DHS reforms as they continue their slow negotiations with the White House, with no clear end in sight.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Illinois, which held primaries on Tuesday, is one of the most gerrymandered states. In 2024, Democrats took 52.78% of the vote there in elections for US House seats. What percentage of such seats did they win?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

💬 Quote Unquote

“If the war were to be extended, it wouldn’t really disrupt the US economy very much at all. It would hurt consumers, and we’d have to think about what we’d have to do about that, but that’s really the last of our concerns right now.”

That’s Trump’s top economic adviser, Kevin Hassett, shamelessly telling American consumers that this administration does not care about them whatsoever.

Surely that messaging will help them get a clean sweep in the midterms!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: The genocide in Gaza continues, as an Israeli airstrike killed a Palestinian man and wounded several others west of Khan Younis today.

🇨🇺 Cuba Xi, Yanqui No: As the US oil embargo bites, and as Trump muses about “taking” the island nation, Cuba is increasingly turning to China to boost its solar power capacity. Considering the geopolitical implications of the shift, one Chinese academic told the Washington Post that while Beijing wanted to be careful “in the backyard of the US … sometimes the economic argument goes with strategic goals.”

🇸🇩 ‘Clock is ticking’: Save the Children warned that “the clock is ticking” as clinics in Sudan could run out of medical supplies within two weeks unless aid shipments disrupted by the US-Israeli war on Iran are swiftly rerouted. The charity’s global supply chain director told Reuters that $600,000 worth of essential medicines are stuck in Dubai, with 400,000 patients in growing need of relief.

🇨🇬 Another octagarian extends his rule: As expected, the Republic of Congo’s President Denis Sassou N’Guesso, the third-longest-serving African president, will extend his rule after winning this week’s tightly-controlled election. Sassou N’Guesso, who has ruled the country for 42 years, won with more than 94% of the vote after the primary opposition parties fielded no candidates, citing a lack of transparency and a crackdown on opposition figures.

😵💫 WTF?!

Who’s going to tell ICE that they’re celebrating a holiday that would’ve never reached America if it weren’t for Irish immigrants?

🧠 Trivia answer: Democrats took 14 of 17 Illinois US House seats — or 82.35%.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

item.title: AIPAC Strikes Back
item.description: On this day in 1969, the Nixon administration began carpet bombing Cambodia. Today, our president is once again a madman and drops bombs “just for fun.” Good morning! Andrew here, taking stock of what happened in last night’s congressional primary races in Illinois. Money might not automatically…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1969, the Nixon administration began carpet bombing Cambodia. Today, our president is once again a madman and drops bombs “just for fun.”

Good morning! Andrew here, taking stock of what happened in last night’s congressional primary races in Illinois. Money might not automatically buy elections – but, boy, are Democratic voters gonna get conned if they listen to any of AIPAC’s super PACs about which candidates are big-time supporters of Medicare for All and which left-wing candidates are actually secret Republicans.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Tuesday’s elections showcase America’s acute democracy crisis, Israel knows protesters in Iran will be “slaughtered” if they listen to Israel and rise up, Graham Platner faces his first attack ad, and Chief Justice John Roberts wishes he could tell the fascist he made president to stop criticizing the Supreme Court – but is it really safe to upset him?

What We’re Up Against

The week began with an NBC News poll finding that 67% of Democrats say their sympathies lie with Palestinians more than Israelis – a giant shift from 2013, when only 18% said the same.

Public opinion is no small matter. It’s not necessarily what decides elections, though, in an era of unprecedented, unlimited dark money and super PAC spending. To be sure, US policymaking has had no correlation with what Americans want for years, at least – the seminal study showing this was published back in 2014. But America’s democracy crisis is only getting more acute, as the ultra-rich and well-heeled interest groups have been given the greenest light, legally speaking, to mess with our elections.

Today, the American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC), the US pro-Israel lobby, can drop $22 million into four congressional primaries, as it did in Illinois, presenting fake and misleading political arguments to voters, and cynically attacking disfavored candidates from the left, while choosing to talk exclusively about topics that have zero relation to the primary issue that AIPAC and its donors care about: Israel.

AIPAC and its allies, through a series of anodyne-named super PACs, spent $22 million on four Illinois congressional primaries, and won two of them. And while AIPAC’s preferred candidate lost in Illinois’s 9th congressional district, the group still managed to block out the strongly pro-Palestine and Palestinian-American candidate Kat Abughazaleh.

In its campaign against Abughazaleh, who wants to end weapons sales to Israel, an AIPAC-aligned super PAC ran ads saying she “supported Marco Rubio for president, said she was to the right of Donald Trump on Social Security, and her campaign is bankrolled by right-wing donors to Trump and Republicans. Who is the real Kat Abughazaleh? We don’t really know.”

Keep in mind: AIPAC is funded by right-wing Trump donors – and if Abughazaleh, a progressive influencer and former Zeteo contributor, thought any of those things today, or was taking right-wing donor money, AIPAC would not have spent money against her.

But this is the power of the super PAC: add money and you can scream whatever bullshit you want through Americans’ TVs, computers, and iPhones. Your budget for trolling is limited only by the size of your donors’ trusts.

In Abughazaleh’s case, an AIPAC-aligned super PAC called the Chicago Progressive Partnership spent $1.2 million on ads attacking her. The same super PAC also spent $105,000 to prop up Bushra Amiwala – a Muslim candidate whom AIPAC did not want to win – and siphon away support from Abughazaleh.

One of the ads declared Amiwala to be “the real deal,” praising her “economic justice” platform: “Medicare for All, a living wage, student loan forgiveness, real affordable housing options, and yes, making the wealthy pay their fair share.”

The Chicago Progressive Partnership only formed on Jan. 22. It has not yet filed a report detailing its donors.

Abughazaleh finished second to Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss, who was backed by Senator Elizabeth Warren, the Congressional Progressive Caucus, and J Street. Abughazaleh appears to have lost by under four points, while Amiwala finished with about 5% of the vote – a spread that will surely generate angst among the left.

AIPAC initially spent millions in the race backing Laura Fine, who finished third, and opposing Biss, a liberal Zionist who said he wanted to condition aid to Israel.

Late in the race, AIPAC apparently took a lesson from its recent failure in New Jersey, where its super PAC undercut a somewhat pro-Israel, establishment Democrat and helped throw the election to a progressive.

AIPAC changed course, and began focusing on drawing away support from Abughazaleh.

A source close to AIPAC described its thinking in Illinois to Jewish Insider on Monday: “the group’s main goal in the primaries is to prevent six candidates – state Sen. Robert Peters in the 2nd District, activist Kina Collins in the 7th, activist Junaid Ahmed and Hanover Park Trustee Yasmeen Bankole in the 8th and influencer Kat Abughazaleh and Skokie School Board member Bushra Amiwala in the 9th – from being elected, as it believes those candidates would be aligned with the far-left ‘Squad’ on Israel policy issues if elected to Congress.”

That note may involve some goalpost shifting regarding the 9th district, to be sure, but none of these candidates won.

Junaid Ahmed lost by five points after the Chicago Progressive Partnership ran ads with the message: “Ahmed is a multi-millionaire who made his fortune on tech stocks, renting expensive properties, and investing in Elon Musk’s company. Ahmed consulted for a pharmaceutical company accused of jacking up prices for life-saving medicine, and he worked for a firm that advised fossil fuel companies as climate change worsened. Junaid Ahmed. He got rich. We got screwed.”

Ahmed, who had between $1,000 and $15,000 invested in Tesla as of 2022, ran on a firmly progressive platform, with support from Senator Bernie Sanders. He backed Medicare for All, wanted to leverage “Medicare’s buying power to bring down prescription drug prices for everyone,” and pushed for “a path to 100% clean energy.”

He also called Israel’s genocide in Gaza a genocide – so AIPAC’s super PAC set out to destroy him from the left by trolling the voters in his district.

Democratic voters may have moved left on Israel and Palestine. But a Democratic Party so dependent on dark money hasn’t followed them. This is what a crisis of representative democracy looks like.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iranian officials said last week that at least 1,444 people have been killed and nearly 20,000 injured in the US-Israeli strikes – the latest official updated figures from the country. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency today put the death toll at more than 3,000, including over 1,354 civilian deaths.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 912 people, including more than 110 children, in Lebanon, according to yesterday’s figures from the country’s health ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: The death toll in Israel rose to at least 14 after an elderly couple was killed in an Iranian strike in a Tel Aviv suburb.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region.

And in more Iran war news…

Senior Iranian officials killed: Israel said today that an Israeli attack killed Iranian Intelligence Minister Esmail Khatib (Iran has yet to comment on the claim). Israel’s claim comes after Iran confirmed late yesterday its security chief, Ali Larijani, and General Gholam Reze Soleimani, head of the volunteer Basij force, were killed in Israeli airstrikes.

Lebanon latest: Israel intensified its bombardment of Lebanon today, hitting central Beirut with some of the most intense strikes in that part of the capital in decades. At least 10 people were killed in Israeli strikes that targeted residential buildings. In southern Lebanon, Israel has reportedly killed several others, including a paramedic. Meanwhile, Israel acknowledged today that its forces fired on UN peacekeeping positions earlier this month in an attack that injured Ghanaian peacekeepers.

Israel predicts ‘slaughter’: Senior Israeli officials told their US counterparts that although Israel is publicly calling for a popular uprising in Iran, it believes protesters will “get slaughtered,” the Washington Post reported, citing a State Department cable.

Trump needs no one: After world leaders loudly rejected US efforts to build a naval coalition to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, Trump now claims he never needed the help of NATO or other partner countries. “WE DON’T NEED THE HELP OF ANYONE!” he declared.

Hunger risk rises: The UN World Food Program warned that almost 45 million more people could face acute food security or worse if the Iran conflict does not end by the middle of the year and if oil stays above $100 a barrel.

Military matters: US officials told Reuters that a fire aboard the US aircraft carrier Gerald R. Ford caused nearly 200 smoke-related injuries and prompted the giant ship to temporarily pull into port during its deployment in the Red Sea.

Mills Goes Negative in Maine

Down in the polls with less than three months left in the Maine Senate primary, Democratic Governor Janet Mills unleashed a blistering attack ad against Graham Platner – confirming the veteran and oyster farmer is, indeed, the frontrunner in the race to take on Republican Senator Susan Collins.

The ad features a speaker whose gravelly voice sounds eerily similar to Platner (Mills’s campaign says it was taped by a voice actor, and wasn’t made with AI). The speaker reads from ugly, misogynistic Reddit posts that the veteran and oyster farmer made in 2013. The ad shows several women responding to the posts, and ends with footage of Platner shirtless, sporting the infamous skull-and-crossbones tattoo – the one resembling a Nazi symbol – that he had covered up last year.

“When I read through my old internet posts when they resurfaced six months ago, I was horrified,” Platner said at a press conference with supporters Tuesday. “I did not recognize in them myself or the man that I am today. I didn’t recognize myself in this person that was struggling to find meaning and posted awful things 13 years ago. I am sorry. But it does not in any way reflect who I am today or the beliefs that I hold.”

🗞️ What You Need to Know

‘Dangerous’ president: Speaking in Houston, Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts seemed to clap back against Trump’s recent attacks by saying: “Personally directed hostility is dangerous and it’s got to stop.” Trump’s latest tantrum came after the Supreme Court struck down his illegal tariffs, leading the president to call the Roberts court “inept and embarrassing.”

Jersey rules: District court Judge Zahid Quraishi reached his wits’ end with the New Jersey US attorney’s office during a hearing, demanding answers on former interim US attorney (and Trump lawyer) Alina Habba’s ongoing involvement in the office before ultimately ejecting senior prosecutor Mark Coyne from the courtroom. Judge Quraishi then called for three officials to testify about who’s really running the NJ office.

Bondi to the front: House Oversight Chair James Comer subpoenaed Attorney General Pam Bondi to testify as part of the committee’s investigation into “possible mismanagement” of the investigation into Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell. Both Democrats and Republicans pushed for the move.

Markwayne his words: Oklahoma GOP Senator Markwayne Mullin will face the Senate Homeland Security Committee today, for his hearing to replace Kristi Noem as DHS Secretary, as the department grinds through a fifth week in shutdown. Democrats are likely to press Mullin on demands for DHS reforms as they continue their slow negotiations with the White House, with no clear end in sight.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Illinois, which held primaries on Tuesday, is one of the most gerrymandered states. In 2024, Democrats took 52.78% of the vote there in elections for US House seats. What percentage of such seats did they win?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

💬 Quote Unquote

“If the war were to be extended, it wouldn’t really disrupt the US economy very much at all. It would hurt consumers, and we’d have to think about what we’d have to do about that, but that’s really the last of our concerns right now.”

That’s Trump’s top economic adviser, Kevin Hassett, shamelessly telling American consumers that this administration does not care about them whatsoever.

Surely that messaging will help them get a clean sweep in the midterms!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: The genocide in Gaza continues, as an Israeli airstrike killed a Palestinian man and wounded several others west of Khan Younis today.

🇨🇺 Cuba Xi, Yanqui No: As the US oil embargo bites, and as Trump muses about “taking” the island nation, Cuba is increasingly turning to China to boost its solar power capacity. Considering the geopolitical implications of the shift, one Chinese academic told the Washington Post that while Beijing wanted to be careful “in the backyard of the US … sometimes the economic argument goes with strategic goals.”

🇸🇩 ‘Clock is ticking’: Save the Children warned that “the clock is ticking” as clinics in Sudan could run out of medical supplies within two weeks unless aid shipments disrupted by the US-Israeli war on Iran are swiftly rerouted. The charity’s global supply chain director told Reuters that $600,000 worth of essential medicines are stuck in Dubai, with 400,000 patients in growing need of relief.

🇨🇬 Another octagarian extends his rule: As expected, the Republic of Congo’s President Denis Sassou N’Guesso, the third-longest-serving African president, will extend his rule after winning this week’s tightly-controlled election. Sassou N’Guesso, who has ruled the country for 42 years, won with more than 94% of the vote after the primary opposition parties fielded no candidates, citing a lack of transparency and a crackdown on opposition figures.

😵💫 WTF?!

Who’s going to tell ICE that they’re celebrating a holiday that would’ve never reached America if it weren’t for Irish immigrants?

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Reza Pahlavi's Movement Is Dangerous. I've Seen It Up Close
item.description: A note from our Editor-in-Chief: This isn’t a voice you normally see on Zeteo. Alireza Nader is a former fellow at the neoconservative, hawkishly pro-Israel Foundation for the Defense of Democracies (FDD) and an Iranian who supports regime change in Iran. He was also once friendly with Reza…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
A note from our Editor-in-Chief:

This isn’t a voice you normally see on Zeteo. Alireza Nader is a former fellow at the neoconservative, hawkishly pro-Israel Foundation for the Defense of Democracies (FDD) and an Iranian who supports regime change in Iran. He was also once friendly with Reza Pahlavi, but he now believes the son of the last shah of Iran is fronting a dangerous, anti-democratic movement. While Zeteo does not share all of the views expressed in this article, we believe this is a rarely-reported perspective on Pahlavi and his supporters and, therefore, this is an important piece.

-Mehdi

It was in 2017 that I first met Reza Pahlavi, the son of the late shah of Iran, at an Italian restaurant in Potomac, Maryland, near where he lived. Our meeting took place right after the mass protests that year began in Iran and after I had left my job at the RAND Corporation, freeing me to advocate for democracy in Iran. I had been particularly impressed with Pahlavi’s passionate defense of the protesters during an interview on Al Jazeera. I had never been a fan of the monarchy in Iran, but I wanted to meet him.

He was an affable and down-to-earth man who genuinely seemed to care about Iran. We spoke at length about democracy and freedom for all Iranians. I believed in Pahlavi’s talk of democracy because it was backed by action at the time. A broad coalition of liberal democrats and even progressives had gathered around him, in addition to the usual constitutional monarchists who had always been the core of his support base.

But Pahlavi’s movement morphed into something much darker and more dangerous over the years, especially after he effectively dropped out of a coalition formed by eight prominent Iranian opposition members at Georgetown University in 2023 and declared himself the sole leader of the Iranian opposition. A few months later, I had lost all faith in the former crown prince and his movement, especially after I observed his staffers plot against other opponents of the regime and treat even their allies with contempt and derision.

To understand how extremist and increasingly violent the Pahlavist movement has become, look no further than the recent murder of Canadian-Iranian democracy activist Masood Masjoody in British Columbia.

Masjoody, a former supporter of Pahlavi, had become a fierce critic of him and his movement. The two people who have been charged with Masjoody’s murder, authorities say, were known to him. Before his death, Masjoody claimed that the two suspects were Pahlavist fanatics who were plotting to kill him using a substance such as poison.

At the time of his death, Masjoody was involved in active litigation in a Vancouver court against Pahlavi and his supporters, including his accused murderers. While the motive for the killing remains a matter of intense public speculation and ongoing police investigation (there’s no evidence at this point that Pahlavi or his advisers were in any way involved), Masjoody’s public profile as a vocal critic of the Pahlavist movement has made the case a focal point for those concerned about the safety of Iranian dissidents, especially after death threats and even physical attacks against Pahlavi critics.

The Pahlavist Threat

Pahlavi attempts to portray himself as a democrat and as a “transitional” leader in a post-regime Iran, but the movement he “leads” has exhibitedalarming behavior.

Pahlavi and his inner circle had been accused by dissidents such as Masjoody of conducting a campaign of intimidation and threats against the rest of the Iranian opposition to silence dissent and position Pahlavi as the only alternative to the current regime.

The Pahlavists are closely supported by elements linked to the Israeli government and Persian-language broadcasts such as Iran International, which has promoted him heavily during the current war and even conducted a defection campaign for him in which potential regime defectors were told to scan a QR Code broadcast by Iran International in order to register their defection in a Google document. Pahlavi later stated that up to 100,000 regime supporters had defected, although no evidence of this claim has emerged.

The cooperation between Pahlavi and the Israeli government appears to have grown stronger since Pahlavi left the Georgetown coalition, and as his movement has taken on the characteristics of illiberal and far-right movements in Israel and across the world.

The Pahlavist movement’s shift toward more rigid, exclusionary rhetoric has been closely associated with advisers such as Amir Etemadi and Saeed Ghasseminejad, the latter of whom is a senior adviser at the Foundation for the Defense of Democracies (FDD). While they are presented as advisers, their behavior is often more akin to handlers who direct the movement’s strategic and ideological path.

Both Etemadi and Ghasseminejad have consistently attacked other members of the opposition, claiming that whoever opposes Pahlavi is an “ethnic separatist” or a “leftist.” Etemadi has even called Iranian journalists at Iran International “terrorists” (before the broadcast became heavily pro-Pahlavi) and attacked dissident Ruhollah Zam, who was eventually lured to Iran and executed by the regime.

Ghasseminejad has stated that “you’re either with Prince Reza Pahlavi or the Islamic Republic,”has written about “the tree branches and lamp posts on Pahlavi Avenue (in Tehran) that have been barren for years,” and has advocated that Israel adopt the death penalty for Palestinians, possibly mimicking such figures as the Israeli far-right Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir.

Much of the harassment and threats are conducted over social media, including on X, but as Masjoody’s case demonstrates, the threats against Pahlavi’s critics are very real.

Masjoody had belonged to an opposition group led by Etemadi called Iran Revival, and was alarmed by the group’s activities.

Masjoody also claimedthat his former associates at Iran Revival were connected to the Revolutionary Guards, a widespread rumor within the Iranian diaspora. Pahlavi’s claims that he had significant support within the regime have only fueled the rumors.

What is more troubling than rumor is the political model reflected in Pahlavi’s published transition plan, which critics, including me, view as overly centralized and weak on independent oversight. The plan, drafted with Ghasseminejad as a lead author and implementer, provides Pahlavi with substantial power rather than building clear checks and balances through credible institutions.

An earlier version also drew criticism for proposing a council whose membership was not publicly disclosed, raising concerns about transparency and accountability. Although that provision was later removed after public backlash, the broader concern remains that the framework did not convincingly answer how power would be restrained during a transition.

Moreover, Ghasseminejad’s employment at FDD and his role in implementing the transition plan are clearly a conflict of interest, as FDD’s mission is not to protect the interests of the Iranian people.

A Useful Israeli Instrument

The war between Israel and Iran demonstrates the utility of Pahlavi for the current Israeli government, which seeks to achieve Israel’s primary national interests, the destruction of Iran’s nuclear and military capabilities, rather than the overthrow of the regime. The Netanyahu government is smart enough to know that Pahlavi is unlikely to become the shah or even the leader of the opposition. Rather, Pahlavi is a useful Israeli instrument in its war against the regime.

For example, Pahlavi called on his supporters to go out into the streets and take over regime institutions in January 2026 after peaceful protests and strikes had broken out in Iran’s bazaars independently of him. The Netanyahu government, more interested in destroying Iran’s military and nuclear capabilities, is likely to have viewed peaceful protests and strikes as an ineffectual strategy. The Israeli government also reportedly knew that any attempt to take over government buildings by unarmed Iranians would lead to a massacre by the regime, though Pahlavi and Iran International urged Iranians to do so anyhow.

The regime reacted to Pahlavi’s calls for a mass uprising by massacring thousands of unarmed Iranian protesters on Jan. 8 and 9, even as Pahlavi and Iran International called for more people to go into the streets on Jan. 11!

Pahlavi has again called for his “Immortal Guard” to take to the streets as the US and Israel bomb military and civilian targets across the country. Hopefully, fewer Iranians will listen to him this time.

A Movement that Cannot Be Rescued

So, what is the solution to the Pahlavist dilemma? The movement has irreparably morphed into a rigid and dogmatic cult of personality. It cannot be rescued. Pahlavi is a hostage of his own inner circle and has become a tool of the Netanyahu government. But he also deserves full blame for his own actions, which have divided the Iranian opposition and induced an atmosphere of fear among the diaspora, even in Washington, DC.

Pahlavi has been repeatedly warned to cease his political activities by friends and associates, but he is likely to continue on his disastrous path as long as he remains the center of attention and applause.

Masjoody was one of the few people in the diaspora who was openly critical of Pahlavi and wanted Iranians to know the truth about his fanatical cult.

I was in contact with him before his murder; he even used my research on monarchists in his court testimony against Pahlavi.

Pahlavi continues to present himself to global audiences as Iran’s savior. Masjoody knew better and tried very hard to make everyone see the truth. When will the world see what he saw?

Alireza Nader is an independent scholar of Iran and the Middle East based in Washington, DC.

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.
