From softball CBS interviews, to the New York Times burying the attack on a girls school on page 11, Mehdi says leading media…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
We saw it with Gaza and we’re seeing it again in Iran. Legacy media is failing us when it comes to holding power to account for the devastating US-Israeli war in Iran. From softball CBS interviews, to the New York Times burying the attack on a girls school on page 11, Mehdi says leading media outlets are “happily manufacturing consent for this devastating and illegal war.”

But at Zeteo, we’re leading with the “blunt honesty and moral clarity” you’re searching for. Listen to Mehdi play out exactly how Zeteo is pushing back against corporate media and why more than ever your subscriber support matters.

item.title: The MAGA Elite Is Being Torn Apart by Trump’s War in Iran
item.description: A counterterrorism official with ties to domestic extremists resigned from Donald Trump’s administration on Tuesday, marking the first major departure over the US-Israeli war in Iran that has exposed a fissure in the broader MAGA coalition and its elite influencers and policy hands. Joe Kent,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded: A counterterrorism official with ties to domestic extremists resigned from Donald Trump’s administration on Tuesday, marking the first major departure over the US-Israeli war in Iran that has exposed a fissure in the broader MAGA coalition and its elite influencers and policy hands. Joe Kent, director of the National Counterterrorism Center, announced his exit on Twitter after just seven months on the job. “I cannot in good conscience support the ongoing war in Iran,” he wrote. “Iran posed no imminent threat to our nation, and it is clear that we started this war due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby.” Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene, an ex-Trump confidant and one of the most outspoken conservative critics of Trump’s illegal war, immediately rallied behind Kent, along with commentators Tucker Carlson and Candace Owens. “They are going to lie about Joe Kent and try to discredit him. Do not believe the lies!” Greene tweeted. “Joe Kent is a great American hero deployed 11 times and a Gold Star Husband!” His service aside, we must note that Kent never should have been in this position in the first place, given his extensive ties to far-right extremists, his own noxious policy positions, and his support for Trump’s anti-democratic lies about the 2020 election – though none of these are considered bad things in the Trump administration. And Kent was, it seems, fine with being part of Team Trump during this president’s other wars of aggression, both at home and abroad. Now that he’s left the cult, Trumpworld is laying into Kent. Former senior Trump White House aide Taylor Budowich called Kent a “crazed egomaniac,” adding: “This isn’t some principled resignation – he just wanted to make a splash before getting canned. What a loser.” Trump attacked Kent on his way out as well. “I always thought he was weak on security,” Trump said Tuesday of the man he appointed as his top counterterrorism official. “When I read the statement, I realized that it’s a good thing that he’s out.” Opposing ‘Endless Wars’ The Trump White House would like the American public to believe that this Kent fellow is just a disgruntled, useless, appointee – and a persistent leaker who should have been fired – and not representative of a broader discontent among MAGAville’s upper echelons. They also want you to believe that everyone who matters in the administration is fully supportive of this president’s war and that there’s no daylight among the Cabinet level. This simply isn’t true. One reason it’s not true is because for it to be true, Cabinet members would have had to be for the war before Trump started it. According to various sources with direct knowledge of the matter and others briefed on the situation, shockingly few at the highest levels – save for President Trump – were gung-ho about launching the war in its run-up. Instead, many prominent members of Trump’s family, outside-adviser orbit, and the Trump-Vance administration (including Vice President JD Vance) defaulted to a go-along-to-get-along position. In internal deliberations, including ones with the president in the room, they sat on the fence. They didn’t push too hard in any particular direction. They just wanted to signal that they would do what Trump wanted, and that once he made the call, they’d work overtime to sell it. And with the war having entered its third, horrifying week, several top Trump officials and Cabinet members, several of the sources say, have only grown more pessimistic about the US’s chances in achieving any real or worthwhile “victory” in the conflict. Some high-ranking personnel whisper to one another about how they are utterly convinced that the war will damage the GOP’s already tenuous-at-best chances in the 2026 midterms – election outcomes that Trump himself stresses are absolutely crucial to the fate of his political agenda. Vanishingly few, however, are talking about quitting in protest. “I’m not going anywhere,” one senior administration official tells Zeteo, adding that the war was “a bad idea” and “a lot of people” in the government don’t think it’s going well and are unhappy that the war is being prioritized right now over other MAGA domestic policy initiatives. (Read: Trump’s ethnic-cleansing campaign.) Extremist Ties As various conservative factions weigh in on Kent’s opposition to the Iran war, they are all glossing over one key detail – that his history of cozying up to domestic extremists and amplifying conspiracy theories should have disqualified him from being appointed to his post to begin with. At his confirmation hearing, Kent – a 2020 election denier – repeated a ridiculous conspiracy theory that federal agents helped plan and direct the Jan. 6 riot at the US Capitol. He has also faced heat for his ties to alt-right white nationalists, including Holocaust denier Nick Fuentes; Joey Gibson, founder of the Christian nationalist group Patriot Prayer; and Proud Boy Graham Jorgenson, whom he paid $11,000 for consulting on Kent’s 2022 failed congressional campaign. Like many top Trump officials, Kent previously claimed that Trump would “end the wars” upon being elected president again in 2024. When Trump picked Tulsi Gabbard as his director of national intelligence, Kent applauded the move, writing that “Tulsi understands how to defend our nation by not getting us involved in endless wars.” As director of the National Counterterrorism Center, Kent served as a top aide to Gabbard. Unlike Kent, Gabbard has not handed in her resignation and remains a top official within the administration. She has had virtually nothing to say about Trump’s war, even though he is waging the exact type of war she’s always spoken out against. On Wednesday, Gabbard registered extremely measured support for Trump’s war in Iran, with a tweet that read like it was written at gunpoint. “Donald Trump was overwhelmingly elected by the American people to be our President and Commander in Chief,” she wrote. “As our Commander in Chief, he is responsible for determining what is and is not an imminent threat, and whether or not to take action he deems necessary to protect the safety and security of our troops, the American people and our country.” She added, “After carefully reviewing all the information before him, President Trump concluded that the terrorist Islamist regime in Iran posed an imminent threat and he took action based on that conclusion. Gabbard for years railed against US-led wars in the Middle East – and she was adamantly opposed to the notion of a war with Iran. During her 2020 campaign, she even sold T-shirts proclaiming in big, bold letters: “No War With Iran.” During the 2024 campaign, she billed Trump as “a man who wants to end wars, not start them.” Base on Board Kent’s departure signals the deepening rift within the MAGA elite over Trump’s war of choice in the Middle East. Axios recently reported that “some in [Trump’s] inner circle have what one official called ‘buyer’s remorse’ – growing fears that attacking Iran was a mistake.” This is partially true in the sense that they very much went along with it – but few, if any, bought into the idea that it was a good idea, as Trump, Lindsey Graham, Sean Hannity, and Israel’s Benjamin Netanyahu did. Today, that dynamic persists. There are next to zero profiles in courage to be found. The most senior levels of the federal government are still dutifully carrying out what the Mad King commands, even if they know it’s the orders of an increasingly Mad King. And yet, the discontent is real. If this war stretches on much longer, growing intra-MAGA displeasure and frustrations will only continue to bubble up to the surface, no matter how much the Trump White House spins the public on how All Is Well. One Trump adviser tells Zeteo they are slightly surprised there haven’t been more resignations at this point, beyond Kent’s, given the internal consternations, and that they expect there to be more if this war goes “sideways.” But this isn’t a hypothetical scenario. In many ways, Trump’s war is already going sideways – and signs point to things getting worse. Trump’s illegal war in Iran might be causing discontent among the MAGA elite and prominent right-wing influencers, like Tucker Carlson and Candace Owens. And it might be causing Trump to bleed political support with the independents who helped him win in 2024. Trump’s base, however, is another story. While a majority of Americans oppose Trump’s handling of the war, 84% of Republican voters support it, according to a Quinnipiac University poll released March 9. A recent average of polls by Nate Silver’s team found that 40% of Americans approve of Trump’s war in Iran – but 77% of Republicans approve of his conflict. Still, there are some in the Trump administration who are predicting that this war could last even into the final months of the year. And there are, sources stress to Zeteo, senior Trump officials who practically take it as a given that this Republican approval number will go down, if Trump’s war begins to turn into a protracted quagmire.
